summary: Jeff Atwood explores why programmers consistently produce poor user interfaces, using Battlefield 2's pregame UI as a launching point. He argues that while GUI builders make interface creation appear simple, truly great UIs cannot be automated or generated. The core problem is that programmers are trained to think code-first rather than user-first, starting with command-line programs in university that emphasize algorithms over usability. Atwood advocates for 'UI First Development' where the interface is designed before implementation begins, though he notes this approach remains uncommon in the industry.
key_points: GUI builders create a false impression that UI design is easy, when in fact great UIs require human creativity that cannot be automated
Server-side programming is actually easier to master because it's well-defined and repetitive, making it automatable
Computer science education trains programmers to think code-first through command-line programs, leaving them unprepared for UI design
The tools for creating UIs work well for simple cases but become inadequate for complex user scenarios
Designing the UI first before writing code is essential for end-user software but rarely practiced
money_quotes: They might be able to generate a rudimentary GUI, but a really GREAT GUI cannot be automated.

When you're working on end-user software, and it doesn't matter if you're working on a web app, adding a feature to an existing application, or working on a plug-in for some other application, you need to design the UI first.
That's the secret of server-side programming: it is very well-defined and repetitive. Thus, it can be automated.
We learned how to implement efficient algorithms for common computer science problems, but we never learned how to design a good UI.
Forms designers are great when you're working within the problem domain that forms are intended to solve, but once you step outside those problem domains, the work gets much harder.
key_insight: Great user interfaces cannot be automated or code-generated—they require deliberate upfront design by people who think about users first and implementation second.
